commonMistakes: Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.
Mutating shared data when the task expects a new value or explicit ownership.
Treating browser APIs and language features as the same layer.
Choosing an abstraction from habit rather than required operations.

contentAuthority.canonical: New JS2Next Release 1 curriculum and independently authored content.
contentAuthority.courseTranscripts: Coverage and sequencing research only; never learner-facing authority.
contentAuthority.legacyProjectContent: Supplementary internal reference only; excluded from runtime unless explicitly re-authored and approved.

learningObjectives.text: Explain how the dom is represented using a new example and justify the result.
Explain event bubbling using a new example and justify the result.
Explain event capturing using a new example and justify the result.
Explain event delegation implications using a new example and justify the result.

mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **How the DOM is represented** → **Event bubbling** → **Event capturing** → **Event delegation implications**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

choices.feedback: How the DOM is represented is best understood as follows: The DOM is a tree of node objects connected through parent, child, and sibling relationships. Traversal APIs expose those relationships.
choices.label: The DOM is a tree of node objects connected through parent, child, and sibling relationships. Traversal APIs expose those relationships.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of How the DOM is represented.
choices.label: A closure keeps access to the lexical environment in which a function was created, even after the outer call has returned.

choices.label: Describe where data originates, who owns it, how it changes, and how the UI observes those changes. Explicit flow prevents hidden state and scattered updates.

choices.feedback: Event bubbling is best understood as follows: During bubbling, an event travels from its target toward ancestors. Ancestors can observe descendant events, enabling delegation.
choices.label: During bubbling, an event travels from its target toward ancestors. Ancestors can observe descendant events, enabling delegation.

questions.explanation: Event bubbling is best understood as follows: During bubbling, an event travels from its target toward ancestors. Ancestors can observe descendant events, enabling delegation.
questions.hint: Focus on what the concept changes, enables, or constrains—not only on its syntax.
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains “Event bubbling” in the context of DOM Internals and Event Propagation?

choices.feedback: Event capturing is best understood as follows: Capturing is the earlier phase from ancestors toward the target. Most application handlers use bubbling, but capture can observe an event first.
choices.label: Capturing is the earlier phase from ancestors toward the target. Most application handlers use bubbling, but capture can observe an event first.

sections.body: The DOM is a tree of node objects connected through parent, child, and sibling relationships. Traversal APIs expose those relationships.
sections.title: How the DOM is represented

sections.body: During bubbling, an event travels from its target toward ancestors. Ancestors can observe descendant events, enabling delegation.
sections.title: Event bubbling

sections.body: Capturing is the earlier phase from ancestors toward the target. Most application handlers use bubbling, but capture can observe an event first.
sections.title: Event capturing

sections.body: Delegation uses one ancestor listener and identifies the relevant descendant from the event target. It works well for dynamic lists but needs boundary checks.
sections.title: Event delegation implications

sections.explanation: One ancestor listener handles descendant buttons through bubbling and `closest`.
sections.title: Worked example

summary: DOM Internals and Event Propagation connects How the DOM is represented, Event bubbling, Event capturing.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.
title: DOM Internals and Event Propagation

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

whyThisMatters: Learners frequently use dom internals and event propagation without a clear model of why it behaves as it does; this lesson makes that model explicit. It belongs to the **DOM Internals and Script Loading** module.
